overall_summary: CAISO generally supports the ANOPR's reform principles, endorsing the 20 MW threshold, coordinated study processes, protection facility requirements, and expedited treatment for curtailable loads. CAISO asks for flexibility to align any final rule with its existing regional processes and Order No. 2023 procedures, and urges the Commission to require detailed load models and real-time telemetry from large loads to protect reliability.

quotes.text: Reforms grounded in the existing rules and procedures applicable to large, transmission-connected generators can be a pragmatic approach that can benefit the broader industry by providing consistency and clarity in the interconnection process for large loads.
quotes.concern: CAISO endorses using generator interconnection rules as the template for large load reforms

quotes.text: The 20 MW threshold has proven to be a reliable figure to assess the risk a generation or storage facility represents to the transmission grid.
quotes.concern: CAISO supports the 20 MW threshold as a clear, workable demarcation

quotes.text: much higher MW thresholds may incentivize developers to create multiple facilities each right under the limit to avoid jurisdiction, rather than a single facility that benefits the facility and the grid from economy of scale.
quotes.concern: Higher thresholds risk gaming by developers splitting facilities to stay under the limit

quotes.text: Providing a single study process for large load, hybrid, and generating facilities may simplify many aspects for transmission providers and developers.  At the same time, it could introduce new complexities to the interconnection study process and transmission planning process, both of which have been significantly reformed by recent Commission orders.
quotes.concern: A unified study process has simplification benefits but may add complexity to recently reformed processes

quotes.text: The longstanding approach of enabling transmission providers to propose tailored processes to meet regional needs under the independent entity standard will allow transmission providers to tailor their studies to ensure the orderly and timely interconnection of large loads.
quotes.concern: CAISO urges the final rule to preserve regional flexibility for study processes

quotes.text: The CAISO supports this proposal, and recommends that if the Commission ultimately mandates specific reforms on this area, that any final rule enable transmission providers to align the large load processes with the processes transmission providers have established in complying with Order No. 2023.
quotes.concern: CAISO asks that study deposit and readiness requirements align with existing Order No. 2023 procedures

quotes.text: Hybrid facilities that have strict, unwavering protections like circuit breakers at their point of interconnection should be allowed to have network upgrades based on their net rights requested.  This will save ratepayers and developers from unnecessary costs and help to avoid long construction timelines.
quotes.concern: Net-rights-based study for hybrids is appropriate only when hard physical protections are in place

quotes.text: Any final rule should clarify that transmission providers still must have detailed modeling information to 'study' the full load and generating capabilities of each individual part of the facility.
quotes.concern: Studies must use gross component data even when net-rights approach is used for upgrade sizing

quotes.text: The CAISO believes this principle is among the most critical to establish.  As NERC has already demonstrated, large loads can affect frequency, voltage, and power quality just as large generators do.
quotes.concern: Protection facility requirements are the most important reform principle because large loads can destabilize the grid

quotes.text: The CAISO also recommends that any reform include the requirement that large loads provide load profiles and expected behavior models as part of the interconnection process.
quotes.concern: Large loads should submit detailed behavioral models as a condition of interconnection

quotes.text: large loads should be required to submit detailed steady-state, dynamic (RMS), and, for certain facilities, electromagnetic transient (EMT) models reflecting expected demand behavior, similar to the modeling requirements currently applied to inverter-based generators.
quotes.concern: EMT and RMS modeling requirements for large loads should mirror those for inverter-based generators

quotes.text: The CAISO recommends that any final rule require large loads and hybrid facilities to provide transmission providers with real-time telemetry at the component level.
quotes.concern: Real-time component-level telemetry from large loads is necessary for grid visibility

quotes.text: The CAISO recommends that any final rule should establish distinct 'lanes' based on the level of dispatch tools the transmission provider will have--all, some, or none.
quotes.concern: Expedited interconnection should be tiered by degree of curtailability, not a binary on/off

quotes.text: being curtailable on some hours or on some days or for only a portion of the load or generating capacity should not allow a facility to avail itself of the same expedited process as completely curtailable facilities.
quotes.concern: Partial curtailability should not qualify for the same fast-track treatment as full curtailability

quotes.text: the CAISO requests that any final rule provide clear guidance on cost responsibility for hybrid facilities: Separating which network upgrades were required by a generator and which network upgrades were required by a co-located large load is impractical, if at all possible for some types of upgrades.
quotes.concern: Cost allocation for hybrid facility upgrades is practically difficult and needs a clear rule

quotes.text: NERC should consider developing specific modeling and performance reliability standards for large loads, similar to MOD, PRC, and BAL standards applicable to large generators.
quotes.concern: CAISO calls for new NERC reliability standards tailored to large loads, parallel to generator standards

bins.name: 20 MW Threshold Support
bins.description: CAISO endorses the 20 MW threshold as a clear, already-understood demarcation that mirrors the figure used for generation and storage facilities. CAISO warns that higher thresholds would invite developers to split facilities to avoid jurisdiction.

bins.name: Jurisdiction Boundary Clarity
bins.description: CAISO does not take a position on the underlying legal jurisdictional questions but notes that the threshold level affects whether developers can game the system to avoid federal oversight.

bins.name: Coordinated Study Process
bins.description: CAISO sees merit in a unified study process for loads and generators but cautions it could add complexity to processes recently overhauled by Order No. 2023. CAISO recommends preserving regional flexibility under the independent entity standard.

bins.name: Deposit and Readiness Alignment
bins.description: CAISO supports standardized study deposits and readiness requirements, asking that any final rule allow transmission providers to align new large load procedures with those already accepted under Order No. 2023.

bins.name: Hybrid Net Rights Study
bins.description: CAISO supports sizing network upgrades to net injection or withdrawal rights for hybrids, provided hard physical protection facilities are installed. CAISO clarifies that the full gross capacity of each component must still be modeled for protection and stability studies.

bins.name: Protection and Modeling Requirements
bins.description: CAISO calls protection facility requirements the most critical reform principle. It recommends requiring large loads to submit steady-state, RMS, and EMT behavioral models and to provide real-time component-level telemetry, mirroring standards applied to large generators and inverter-based resources.

bins.name: Tiered Expedited Interconnection
bins.description: CAISO supports expedited treatment for curtailable and dispatchable loads but recommends the final rule create distinct tiers based on the full scope of dispatch tools available. Partial curtailability should not qualify for the same treatment as fully curtailable facilities.

bins.name: Hybrid Upgrade Cost Clarity
bins.description: CAISO supports the 100% cost responsibility principle for loads but asks for explicit guidance on how to allocate costs for hybrid facilities where generator and load upgrade needs cannot be cleanly separated.

bins.name: Study Process Flexibility
bins.description: CAISO consistently supports study reforms while asking for flexibility so transmission providers can align large load processes with their existing Order No. 2023 procedures and regional planning frameworks. Gross component modeling must remain a requirement even under net-rights approaches.

bins.name: Cost Responsibility Rules
bins.description: CAISO supports assigning network upgrade costs to large load customers and endorses net-rights-based upgrade sizing for hybrids with proper protection facilities. CAISO asks for a clear rule on which cost responsibility regime applies to hybrid facilities.

bins.name: Co-location Policy
bins.description: CAISO supports policies that accommodate co-located generation and load, including net-rights studies for hybrids and ancillary service provisions, while calling for clear guidance on cost allocation when generation and load upgrade needs are intertwined.

bins.name: Curtailability Incentives
bins.description: CAISO supports using curtailability and dispatchability as the basis for expedited interconnection. It recommends a tiered lane system so that only fully curtailable facilities receive the most expedited treatment, preserving reliability incentives.

bins.name: CAISO Regional Context
bins.description: CAISO describes its existing collaborative planning framework with the CEC and CPUC, including a 2022 MOU for incorporating large load demand forecasts into transmission planning. CAISO notes this regional model supports the goals of the ANOPR and asks that any rule preserve the independent entity standard.

bins.name: Generator Rules as Template
bins.description: CAISO's central argument is that large loads affect the grid the same way large generators do, so applying existing generator interconnection rules to large loads is a practical and consistent approach.

bins.name: Gross Component Modeling
bins.description: Even when upgrades are sized to net rights, CAISO insists that transmission providers must model the full gross capacity of each generator and load component in a hybrid facility to conduct valid protection and stability studies.

bins.name: Large Load Reliability Risks
bins.description: CAISO catalogues NERC findings on the frequency, voltage, and power quality risks posed by large loads, treating these as the factual basis for why generator-level protection and modeling requirements are necessary.

bins.name: Behavioral Load Models Required
bins.description: CAISO asks that large loads be required to submit steady-state, RMS, and EMT models capturing ramps, start-up sequences, step changes, and harmonic characteristics, mirroring the modeling rules for inverter-based generators.

bins.name: Real-Time Telemetry Mandate
bins.description: CAISO recommends requiring large loads and hybrid facilities to provide transmission providers with real-time component-level telemetry, giving grid operators the same visibility they have for large generators.

bins.name: Tiered Curtailability Lanes
bins.description: CAISO recommends that the final rule establish distinct interconnection lanes tied to the degree of dispatch control the transmission provider will have, ensuring that partial curtailability does not receive the same expedited treatment as full curtailability.

bins.name: Hybrid Cost Allocation Gap
bins.description: CAISO flags a practical problem: for hybrid facilities it is often impossible to separate which network upgrades were driven by the generator versus the load, and asks the Commission to specify which cost responsibility regime governs hybrids.

bins.name: New NERC Standards for Large Loads
bins.description: CAISO recommends NERC develop modeling and performance reliability standards specific to large loads, analogous to the MOD, PRC, and BAL standards that apply to large generators, to ensure consistent treatment across balancing authorities.
